duck-harness-kaggle

Diagnostics for one TAAF benchmark run — a single solver played each game over a fixed number of passes. Plots track score-vs-cost across the run; the table lists every (game, pass) cell, with the movie cell linking to a video of that play-through.

Solver: duck-harness

25 games × 1 pass = 25 runs

Started: 2026-07-18 21:56:31

Ended: 2026-07-19 00:08:46

Duration: 2h 12m 14s

git status
git status:
  tufa-arc-agi-framework           ?           DIRTY  ?                         ?
  ARC3-Inference                   ?           DIRTY  ?                         ?

Standard ARC-AGI3 score. Each completed level contributes min(115, (baseline / actions)² × 100), weighted by level index (level 1 → weight 1, level 2 → 2, …) and normalised by the total weight. Mirrors the engine scorecard.

Per-run stats

runpassesmeanσ across passesSEM
duck-harness-kaggle11.75——

Score vs cumulative tokens

What this curve shows. Reading the curve at x = X tells you the expected score per game when the method is run at an average per-game budget of X tokens. Slow games may use more, fast games less; the average works out to X.

What-if: per-level token caps

Each curve shows what the score-vs-tokens curve would have been if every level had been stopped at the listed per-level token cap. The cap is on cumulative tokens spent since the most recent level win: cross the cap without winning the level and the lane truncates there with no further credit (full credit is kept for previously-won levels). 100% is the largest token total any single level actually consumed in this run (won or not); the other curves are fractional cuts of that.

Score vs wallclock

Design note: we still have to think about how to plot this exactly when we have some real benchmark data — games run in parallel during the benchmark, so wallclock per game is not just total wallclock / n_games.

Same per-level score formula as the official ARC scorer, but with flat weights [1, 2, 2, …] — level 1 contributes 1×, every later level 2×. Values reaching deeper levels more uniformly than the official 1, 2, 3, … ramp.

Per-run stats

duck-harness-kaggle13.85——

Score vs cumulative tokens

What this curve shows. Reading the curve at x = X tells you the expected score per game when the method is run at an average per-game budget of X tokens. Slow games may use more, fast games less; the average works out to X.

What-if: per-level token caps

Each curve shows what the score-vs-tokens curve would have been if every level had been stopped at the listed per-level token cap. The cap is on cumulative tokens spent since the most recent level win: cross the cap without winning the level and the lane truncates there with no further credit (full credit is kept for previously-won levels). 100% is the largest token total any single level actually consumed in this run (won or not); the other curves are fractional cuts of that.

Score vs wallclock

Design note: we still have to think about how to plot this exactly when we have some real benchmark data — games run in parallel during the benchmark, so wallclock per game is not just total wallclock / n_games.

Raw count of levels completed during the run — no baseline ratio, no partial credit, no normalisation. Games with more levels have a higher possible maximum, so this score is comparable across runs of the same games but not across different game sets.

Per-run stats

runpassesmeanσ across passesSEM

Score vs cumulative tokens

What this curve shows. Reading the curve at x = X tells you the expected score per game when the method is run at an average per-game budget of X tokens. Slow games may use more, fast games less; the average works out to X.

What-if: per-level token caps

Each curve shows what the score-vs-tokens curve would have been if every level had been stopped at the listed per-level token cap. The cap is on cumulative tokens spent since the most recent level win: cross the cap without winning the level and the lane truncates there with no further credit (full credit is kept for previously-won levels). 100% is the largest token total any single level actually consumed in this run (won or not); the other curves are fractional cuts of that.

Score vs wallclock

Design note: we still have to think about how to plot this exactly when we have some real benchmark data — games run in parallel during the benchmark, so wallclock per game is not just total wallclock / n_games.

Sections below this point — per-game cards, per-game drill-downs, and the per-pass-per-game table — always render under the official ARC-AGI score, independent of the variant chosen above.
